1685	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1686	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1687	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1688	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1689	  \[
1690	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1691	  \]
1692	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1693	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1694	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1695	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1696	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1697	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free
1698	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the
1699	  augmentation map \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) onto this image

1700	  presheaf.

1703	  raw-finite-family mirror of this construction (an arbitrary finite family of opens,
1704	  no covering hypothesis), feeding the family form of the {\v C}ech acyclicity lemma.
1705	\end{definition}

1707	\begin{lemma}
1708	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1709	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1710	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1711	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1712	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1713	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1714	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1715	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1716	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1717	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1718	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1719	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1720	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1721	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1722	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1723	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1724	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1725	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1726	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1727	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1728	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1729	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1730	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1731	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1732	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1733	  homology) and jointly conservative: a morphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is an isomorphism
1734	  iff each of its evaluations is an isomorphism, and each evaluation functor preserves
1735	  homology.
1736	\end{lemma}
1737	
1738	\begin{proof}
1739	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1740	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1741	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1742	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1743	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1744	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1745	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1746	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1747	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1748	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1749	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1750	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1751	  quasi-isomorphism.
1752	\end{proof}

1754	\begin{lemma}
1755	
1756	[Sectionwise description of the evaluated free complex]
1757	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
1758	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_X,
1759	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le,
1760	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le,
1761	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_XFam}
1762	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1763	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1764	  % (proof, the sectionwise description of $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$) (read from
1765	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1227--1252).
1766	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
1767	  % if $i \in I_1$. ... If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
1768	  % $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$ and
1769	  % $K^{ext}_p(W) = \bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W)$.
1770	  % This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
1771	  % $(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$."
1772	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1773	  (description of the evaluated complex).}
1774	  Fix an open \(V \subset X\) and write \(I_1 = I_1(V) = \{\,i \in I : V \leq U_i\,\}\)
1775	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex
1776	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} at
1777	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1778	  \[
1779	    K(\mathcal{U})_p(V)
1780	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1781	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1782	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1783	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1784	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),
1785	  \]
1786	  because \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1787	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1788	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1789	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1790	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1791	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1792	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1793	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1794	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1795	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1796	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1797	  is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1798	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).
1799	  % NOTE: Lean assembly of the degreewise iso: (i) evaluation commutes with the
1800	  % coproduct (cechFreeEval_X); (ii) a surviving summand (V \leq U_\sigma) evaluates
1801	  % to \mathcal{O}_X(V) (freeYonedaEval_iso_of_le); (iii) a non-surviving summand
1802	  % is zero (freeYonedaEval_isZero_of_not_le). The cover-agnostic family mirror is
1803	  % cechFreeEval_XFam, used by the family form of the Cech acyclicity lemma.
1804	\end{lemma}
1805	
1806	\begin{proof}
1807	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:cover_structure_presheaf}
1808	  The degreewise identity is the value at \(V\) of the explicit description of the
1809	  free presheaf \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,W)\) recorded in
1810	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free
1811	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-module on \(\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)\), which is
1812	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) if \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise. Applying
1813	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}\;V\) to the coproduct decomposition
1814	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) commutes with the direct sum and
1815	  collapses each summand to \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) or \(0\) according to whether
1816	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\), i.e.\ whether \(\sigma\) factors through \(I_1\); the surviving
1817	  summands are exactly those indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\). The
1818	  evaluated faces are the index-dropping restriction maps, which on the surviving
1819	  summands are identities of \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), so the alternating sum is the
1820	  combinatorial {\v C}ech differential. The augmentation value follows from
1821	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) exactly when some \(U_i\)
1822	  contains \(V\) (\(I_1 \neq \varnothing\)), recorded in
1823	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_structure_presheaf}.
1824	\end{proof}

1846	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
1847	  % is given by
1848	  % $$

1850	  % \sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
1851	  % (-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
1852	  % $$
1853	  % The sum is finite as the element $s$ has finite support.
1854	  % Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
1855	  % $$
1856	  % h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)
1857	  % $$
1858	  % by the rule
1859	  % $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} =
1860	  % \left\{ \begin{matrix}
1861	  % 0 & \text{if} & i_0 \not = i_{\text{fix}} \\
1862	  % s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p + 1}} & \text{if} & i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}
1863	  % \end{matrix} \right.$"
1864	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1865	  (differential and contracting homotopy of the evaluated complex).}
1866	  Fix a nonempty index set \(J\) (in the application \(J = I_1(V)\)), a chosen index
1867	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in J\), and a single coefficient module \(M\). The
1868	  \emph{constant-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech engine} consists of: the
1869	  alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\) on the
1870	  full simplex over \(J\) with coefficients \(M\),
1871	  \[
1872	    (\operatorname{combDifferential} t)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1873	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\, t_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}},
1874	  \]

1876	  \operatorname{combDifferential} = 0\) (\(\operatorname{combDifferential\_comp}\)); the
1877	  \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} homotopy \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\),
1878	  \((\operatorname{combHomotopy}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, u)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1879	  = u_{i_1 \ldots i_{p+1}}\) when \(i_0 = i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) and \(0\) otherwise; and
1880	  the contracting identity \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\)
1881	  (\(\operatorname{combHomotopy\_spec}\)), which yields positive-degree exactness
1882	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_exact}\) and the cocycle-splitting form
1883	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential\_eq\_of\_cocycle}\). The remaining declarations are

1969	    C_p \;=\; \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1970	    \qquad (\operatorname{cechEngineX}),
1971	  \]
1972	  the coproduct taken in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(\mathcal{O}_X(V))\), and with
1973	  differential \(d = \operatorname{cechEngineD}\) given on the injection
1974	  \(\iota_\sigma\) of the index \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)\) by the
1975	  alternating sum of index-drop reindexings,
1976	  \[
1977	    d \circ \iota_\sigma
1978	      \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{p} (-1)^i\, \iota_{\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i}
1979	    \qquad (\operatorname{cechEngineD\_\iota}),
1980	  \]
1981	  i.e.\ the chain transpose of the index-\emph{raise} differential
1982	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) of
1983	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}. Then:
1984	  \begin{itemize}
1985	    \item \(d \circ d = 0\) (\(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_comp}\)), so the family
1986	      assembles into the chain complex \(C_\bullet = \operatorname{ChainComplex.of}\)
1987	      (\(\operatorname{cechEngineComplex}\)).
1988	    \item The \emph{prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)} map
1989	      \(s \mapsto \iota_{\operatorname{Fin.cons}\, i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, \sigma}\)
1990	      (\(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend}\), with its injection computation
1991	      \(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend\_\iota}\)) is a contracting homotopy in
1992	      positive degree: \(s \circ d + d \circ s = \mathrm{id}\)
1993	      (\(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend\_spec}\)).
1994	    \item Hence \(C_\bullet\) is exact in positive degree,
1995	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(\operatorname{cechEngineD}(n+1),
1996	      \operatorname{cechEngineD} n)\) (\(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_exact}\)).
1997	  \end{itemize}
1998	  Four object-half bricks realise the degreewise identification
1999	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong C_p\) consumed by
2000	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_engine_iso}: evaluation commutes with the coproduct;
2001	  the non-surviving summands (those \(\sigma\) with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\)) drop to the
2002	  zero object (\(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
2003	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalEngine\_X}\)); and the surviving index type is reindexed
2004	  by the equivalence
2005	  \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv} : (\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)) \simeq
2006	  \{\, \sigma \;//\; V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma \,\}\),
2007	  where the membership criterion
2008	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma
2009	  \iff \forall k,\ V \leq U_{\sigma(k)}\) is
2010	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\).
2011	
2012	  The parallel \texttt{...Fam} engine declarations pinned above are the cover-agnostic
2013	  raw-finite-family mirror of this construction (an arbitrary finite family of opens,
2014	  no covering hypothesis), feeding the family form of the {\v C}ech acyclicity lemma.
2015	\end{lemma}

2017	\begin{proof}
2018	  \uses{lem:free_cech_engine}
2019	  The complex \(C_\bullet\) is the chain/coproduct dual of the constant-coefficient
2020	  core of Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}. The differential
2021	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) is defined on coproduct injections by the
2022	  alternating-sign index-drop \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove}
2023	  i\); both \(d \circ d = 0\) and the contracting identity
2024	  \(s \circ d + d \circ s = \mathrm{id}\) are proved by extending along the
2025	  injections with \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) and reducing each
2026	  injection-component to the corresponding combinatorial cancellation of
2027	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential\_comp}\) respectively
2028	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\) (the prepend
2029	  \(\operatorname{cechEnginePrepend}\) is the coproduct dual of
2030	  \(\operatorname{combHomotopy}\)). Positive-degree exactness
2031	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD\_exact}\) reads off the contracting identity. The four
2032	  object-half bricks are immediate: evaluation preserves \(\coprod\) and the surviving
2033	  summands are picked out by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) under the criterion
2034	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\), the rest being zero objects
2035	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\)).
2036	\end{proof}

2109	\begin{proof}
2110	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2111	  \emph{Degreewise.} By \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) of
2112	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the
2113	  coproduct,
2114	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_{\sigma :
2115	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0} (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}
2116	  (\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\). Split the index set into the \(\sigma\) landing in
2117	  \(I_1(V)\) and the rest. Each summand with \(\sigma\) valued in \(I_1(V)\) (so
2118	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\)) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) by
2119	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); each remaining summand is the zero
2120	  object by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\). Hence the coproduct
2121	  collapses to \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V) =
2122	  C_p\).
2123	
2124	  \emph{Differential match.} The evaluated differential is \((\operatorname{eval} V)\)
2125	  applied to the alternating face sum
2126	  \(\operatorname{AlternatingFaceMapComplex.objD}(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}
2127	  \mathcal{U})\). The face \(\delta_i\) reindexes the \(\sigma\)-summand by
2128	  \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (the face action of
2129	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}\)); restricted to the
2130	  \(I_1\)-summands this is exactly the index-drop that the engine encodes, and the sign
2131	  \((-1)^i\) is supplied by \(\operatorname{objD}\). The match is verified on the
2132	  coproduct injections by \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\), reusing the
2133	  naturality of \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\); summing with signs
2134	  identifies the evaluated differential with
2135	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\). This is the hard content of the
2136	  identification and the chain/coproduct dual of the cochain coface match in
2137	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}.
2138	
2139	  \emph{The three layers commuted past.} The degreewise iso is the \(3\)-fold
2140	  composite
2141	  \[
2142	    (\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p)
2143	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\;}
2144	      \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0}
2145	        (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))
2146	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\;}
2147	      \coprod_{V \leq U_\sigma} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
2148	      \xrightarrow{\;(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \ldots)^{-1}\;}
2149	      C_p,
2150	  \]
2151	  whose layers are: (1) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) = the comparison
2152	  \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\) expressing that evaluation at
2153	  \(V\) commutes with the coproduct \(\coprod\); (2) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
2154	  which kills the summands with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) (these evaluate to zero objects
2155	  by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\)); (3)
2156	  \((\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \operatorname{survivingEquiv}\
2157	  (\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\, \ldots))^{-1}\), which reindexes the
2158	  surviving summands by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) of
2159	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} and identifies each evaluated summand with the
2160	  single coefficient \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) via \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\).
2161	
2162	  \emph{Naturality past each layer.} The reindex \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) is
2163	  natural in the face map \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\):
2164	  a surviving index \(\sigma\) (i.e.\ \(V \leq U_\sigma\), equivalently
2165	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma\) by
2166	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\)) is carried to a surviving
2167	  \(\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (still \(V \leq U_{\sigma \circ
2168	  \operatorname{succAbove}\, i}\), since dropping a coordinate only weakens the meet),
2169	  so the drop-zeros layer (2) and the whisker layer (3) commute with every face. On a
2170	  surviving summand the evaluated transition \(\operatorname{freeYoneda}.\mathrm{map}
2171	  (\operatorname{homOfLE}\, \ldots)\) collapses to the identity under
2172	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); hence each face \(\delta_i\) matches the
2173	  engine reindexing \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\)
2174	  summand-for-summand, and summing with the \(\operatorname{objD}\) signs \((-1)^i\)
2175	  identifies the evaluated alternating-face differential with
2176	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) (\(= \) the chain transpose of
2177	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\)).
2178	
2179	  \emph{The genuine difficulty: variance.} The free \emph{chain} differential
2180	  \emph{lowers} \(\mathrm{Fin}\)-arity (\(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ
2181	  \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\), faces), whereas
2182	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) \emph{raises} it (it inserts an
2183	  index, summing over \(i \in I_1\)). The two are reconciled because on the finite
2184	  biproduct \(\coprod = \prod\) the insertion/deletion adjunction makes the index-drop
2185	  chain differential the transpose of the index-raise cochain differential; this
2186	  transpose is precisely what \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) of
2187	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} packages, and verifying the commutation amounts to
2188	  the injectionwise check \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) outlined above.
2189	\end{proof}

2351	  \((dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\): the \(j = 0\) term of \(dh\) prepends and then

2536	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
2537	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
2538	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.

2627	\begin{lemma}
2628	[Hom into an injective preserves exactness and quasi-isomorphisms]
2629	  \label{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.preadditiveYoneda_obj_preservesFiniteColimits_of_injective,
2631	    AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_map_preadditiveYoneda_of_injective}
2632	  \uses{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2633	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category and \(I\) an injective object. Then the
2634	  contravariant hom functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I) : \mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}} \to
2635	  \mathrm{Ab}\) is exact: it is automatically left exact (preserves finite limits), and
2636	  injectivity of \(I\) makes it carry epimorphisms to epimorphisms, so it preserves
2637	  finite colimits and hence preserves homology. Consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\)
2638	  carries quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes in \(\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}}\) to
2639	  quasi-isomorphisms of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-cochain complexes. Both statements
2640	  are for a general abelian category, the right generality for reuse in
2641	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The mechanism --- a right adjoint / exact
2642	  contravariant functor preserves the relevant exactness --- is the same homological
2643	  principle behind Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint}.
2644	\end{lemma}
2645	
2646	\begin{proof}
2647	  \uses{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2648	  For injective \(I\), injectivity gives that \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\) carries
2649	  epimorphisms to epimorphisms. Combined with the automatic preservation of finite limits
2650	  (left exactness), the short-exact-sequence criterion shows it preserves finite colimits;
2651	  hence, being also left exact, it preserves homology. A functor that preserves homology
2652	  sends quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes to quasi-isomorphisms degreewise, which is
2653	  the second statement.
2654	\end{proof}

2695	    \end{cases}
2696	  \]
2697	  The positive-degree vanishing holds over an \emph{arbitrary finite family of opens}
2698	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\), with no covering hypothesis: the augmented free {\v C}ech
2699	  resolution is exact \emph{stalkwise} — at a point \(x\) it is the augmented simplicial
2700	  chain complex of the full simplex on \(\{i \mid x \in U_i\}\) with coefficients
2701	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\), which is contractible — and a covering hypothesis only fixes the
2702	  identity of the augmentation target \(\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}\), never the exactness.
2703	  Consequently the lemma applies to any standard covering data of the affine cover system,
2704	  with no restriction to coverings of the whole space.
2705	
2706	  This cover-agnostic form is recorded as \(\operatorname{injective\_cech\_acyclicFam}\),
2707	  parameterized by a raw finite family \(\{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) of opens
2708	  (\(\iota\) finite) carrying \emph{no} covering hypothesis; it consumes the family-form
2709	  resolution quasi-isomorphism \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplex\_quasiIsoFam}\) and is the
2710	  form invoked over the standard covers of an arbitrary distinguished open \(D(f)\) by the
2711	  cover-agnostic affine vanishing statement (Tag 02KG).
2712	\end{lemma}

2737	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_op_identification, lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso, lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2738	  The argument has two independent parts.
2739	
2740	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
2741	  left adjoint to the inclusion
2742	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
2743	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
2744	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
2745	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
2746	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
2747	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
2748	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
2749	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
2750	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2751	
2752	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
2753	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
2754	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
2755	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
2756	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
2757	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2758	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
2759	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
2760	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
2761	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
2762	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
2763	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
2764	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
2765	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
2766	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\).
2767	
2768	  \emph{Categorical assembly.} Concretely, the exactness is produced by the
2769	  opposite-transport bridge. The free {\v C}ech augmentation is a quasi-isomorphism
2770	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}); forming its opposite complex and
2771	  applying \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) keeps it a quasi-isomorphism, since
2772	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as a presheaf of modules (Part 1) and
2773	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) then preserves homology
2774	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}). Transporting source and target of this
2775	  opposite quasi-isomorphism across the opposite-transport isomorphisms
2776	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_op_identification} and
2777	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso}) identifies it with the augmentation
2778	  of the section {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
2779	  \mathcal{I})\) onto the degree-\(0\)-concentrated target \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\).
2780	  The latter has vanishing homology in positive degrees, so the section {\v C}ech complex
2781	  does too. Hence \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
2782	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
2783	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
2784	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
2785	\end{proof}

2855	  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) and (b) each restriction \(s|_{U_i}\) lifts to a section
2856	  \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\); both can be arranged simultaneously because the

2875	\section{Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object}
2876	
2877	The two vanishing lemmas below speak about the \emph{absolute} sheaf cohomology
2878	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module over an open \(U\). Their
2879	dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
2880	identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
2881	sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
2882	\emph{Ext of a corepresenting object} in the single abelian category
2883	\(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\): there is an
2884	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresenting the
2885	sections-over-\(U\) functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\), and we set
2886	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2887	\mathcal{F})\). The corepresenting object is built \emph{without} the extension-by-zero
2888	functor — it is the sheafification of the free module on the representable presheaf
2889	\(\mathbf{y}\,U\) — and the realization keeps the injective module in the \emph{second}
2890	Ext argument, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf with \emph{no} appeal
2891	to restriction of module sheaves. The corepresenting object and its corepresentability
2892	are fixed first, then the following definition fixes the realization, and the Mathlib
2893	dependency anchors after it pin the exact results it rests on.
2894	
2895	\begin{definition}
2896	
2897	\leanok
2898	[Extension-by-zero structure sheaf as a corepresenting object]
2899	  \label{def:jshriek_ou}

2900	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU}
2901	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction,
2902	    lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2903	  \textit{Corepresenting object for sections over an open, built without the
2904	  extension-by-zero functor.}
2905	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subscheme, regarded through the
2906	  representable presheaf \(\mathbf{y}\,U\) on the site of opens of \(X\). The
2907	  \emph{extension-by-zero structure sheaf} \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) is the object of
2908	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) obtained by sheafifying the free
2909	  presheaf of modules on \(\mathbf{y}\,U\):
2910	  \[
2911	    j_!\mathcal{O}_U \;:=\; \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(
2912	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)\bigr)
2913	    \;\in\; X.\mathrm{Modules},
2914	  \]
2915	  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor on the
2916	  representable (the degree-zero generator of the free presheaf complex of
2917	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}) and
2918	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction of
2919	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}. Up to isomorphism this is the classical
2920	  extension-by-zero \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) of the structure sheaf of \(U\) along the open
2921	  immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\); crucially, we build only this \emph{object} as a
2922	  sheafified free module, never the extension-by-zero \emph{functor} \(j_!\). The object
2923	  exists unconditionally: the free presheaf of modules on a representable and the
2924	  sheafification of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are both supplied by Mathlib.
2925	\end{definition}
2926	
2927	\begin{lemma}
2928	
2929	[Corepresentability of global sections over \(U\)]
2930	  \label{lem:jshriek_corepr}
2931	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv, AlgebraicGeometry.sheafificationHomAddEquiv}
2932	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2933	  \textit{The object \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the sections-over-\(U\) functor.}
2934	  For every \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) there is a natural additive
2935	  isomorphism
2936	  \[
2937	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
2938	    \;\cong\;
2939	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \;=\; \mathcal{F}(U),
2940	  \]
2941	  natural in \(\mathcal{F}\). Thus \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the global-sections
2942	  functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
2943	\end{lemma}
2944	
2945	\begin{proof}
2946	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2947	  Compose two natural additive isomorphisms. The sheafification adjunction
2948	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) of
2949	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction} transports corepresentability through
2950	  sheafification:
2951	  \[
2952	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(
2953	      \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)),\, \mathcal{F}
2954	    \bigr)
2955	    \;\cong\;
2956	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2957	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr).
2958	  \]
2959	  The free–Yoneda additive equivalence \(\operatorname{freeYonedaHomAddEquiv}\) of
2960	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} then identifies the right-hand
2961	  presheaf Hom-group with the evaluation \((\iota\mathcal{F})(U) = \mathcal{F}(U) =
2962	  \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\):
2963	  \[
2964	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2965	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr)
2966	    \;\cong\;
2967	    \mathcal{F}(U).
2968	  \]
2969	  Both isomorphisms are additive and natural in \(\mathcal{F}\), so their composite is
2970	  the asserted natural additive isomorphism.
2971	\end{proof}
2972	
2973	\begin{definition}
2974	
2975	\leanok
2976	[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object]
2977	  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
2978	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomology}
2979	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:jshriek_corepr, lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib,
2980	    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib,
2981	    lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib, lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
2982	  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
2983	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
2984	  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Let
2985	  \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U \in X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be the corepresenting object of
2986	  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}. The \emph{absolute sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2987	  over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of this corepresenting object in the single
2988	  abelian category \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
2989	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2990	  \[
2991	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}
2992	      \bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}\bigr),
2993	    \qquad p \geq 0.
2994	  \]
2995	  Both Ext arguments live in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); no restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2996	  along \(j\) is taken. Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
2997	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); the
2998	  \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available unconditionally by
2999	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts the downstream
3000	  dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding Mathlib results:
3001	  \begin{enumerate}
3002	  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
3003	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
3004	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), by the
3005	    degree-zero Ext comparison (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); composing
3006	    with the corepresentability isomorphism
3007	    \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
3008	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} gives \(H^0(U,
3009	    \mathcal{F}) \cong \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
3010	  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
3011	    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\), one has
3012	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) by
3013	    Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Since \(\mathcal{I}\) is exactly
3014	    the second argument of \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{I})\),
3015	    this yields \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly: the short exact sequence stays
3016	    in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), no restriction is taken, and
3017	    restriction-preserves-injectives is not needed.
3018	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
3019	    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and the
3020	    \emph{fixed} first argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact
3021	    sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of
3022	    the groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext
3023	    class of the extension.
3024	  \end{enumerate}
3025	\end{definition}
3026	
3027	\begin{definition}
3028	[Ext bifunctor in an abelian category]
3029	  \label{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}
3030	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext}
3031	  \mathlibok
3032	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
3033	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
3034	  For an abelian category \(C\) with a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure and objects \(X, Y
3035	  \in C\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), Mathlib provides the Ext group
3036	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^n_C(X, Y)\), functorial (contravariantly in \(X\), covariantly in
3037	  \(Y\)) and equipped with a composition pairing.
3038	\end{definition}
3039	
3040	\begin{lemma}
3041	[Unconditional availability of \(\mathrm{HasExt}\)]
3042	  \label{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
3043	  \lean{CategoryTheory.HasExt.standard}
3044	  \mathlibok
3045	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
3046	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
3047	  Every abelian category \(C\) carries a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure; in particular the
3048	  module-sheaf category \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\) admits the Ext bifunctor of
3049	  Definition~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib} unconditionally.
3050	\end{lemma}
3051	
3052	\begin{lemma}
3053	[Degree-zero Ext is the Hom-group]
3054	  \label{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}
3055	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.homEquiv₀,
3056	    CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀}
3057	  \mathlibok
3058	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
3059	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
3060	  For objects \(A, Y\) of an abelian category with \(\mathrm{HasExt}\), there is a
3061	  natural (additive) isomorphism \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(A, Y) \cong (A \to Y)\) between
3062	  degree-zero Ext and the Hom-group. Applied to the first argument \(A =
3063	  j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), this identifies \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
3064	  \mathcal{F})\) with
3065	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), which the
3066	  corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} in turn identifies
3067	  with \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
3068	\end{lemma}

3070	\begin{lemma}
3071	[Ext vanishes on injectives in positive degree]
3072	  \label{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}
3073	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.eq_zero_of_injective}
3074	  \mathlibok

3076	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.EnoughInjectives}).}
3077	  Let \(C\) be an abelian category with \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) and \(I \in C\) an injective
3078	  object. Then every element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(X, I)\) is zero, i.e.
3079	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(X, I) = 0\) for all \(X\) and all \(n \geq 0\). The
3080	  vanishing requires only the \emph{second} argument to be injective.

3093	  Let \(C\) be an abelian category with \(\mathrm{HasExt}\), let \(0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to
3094	  S_3 \to 0\) be a short exact sequence in \(C\), and fix a first argument \(X \in C\).

3096	  with connecting homomorphism \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n_0}(X, S_3) \to
3097	  \operatorname{Ext}^{n_1}(X, S_1)\) (for \(n_0 + 1 = n_1\)) given by composition with
3098	  the Ext class of the extension. Mathlib packages the exactness both as the

3100	  \(\operatorname{covariantSequence\_exact}\) and as the three step-lemmas

3139	[Naturality of \(H^0 \cong \Gamma\) in the coefficient sheaf]
3140	  \label{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero_natural}
3141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomologyZeroAddEquiv_naturality,
3142	    AlgebraicGeometry.homEquiv₀_comp_mk₀,
3143	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaHomEquiv_naturality,
3144	    AlgebraicGeometry.sheafificationHomAddEquiv_naturality,
3145	    AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv_nat,
3146	    AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv_naturality}

3148	  \textit{Project-bespoke obligation.}
3149	  The isomorphism \(H^0(U, -) \cong \Gamma(U, -)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_zero} is
3150	  natural in the coefficient sheaf: for a morphism \(g : \mathcal{F}_1 \to \mathcal{F}_2\) in
3151	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) the square
3152	  \[
3153	    \begin{array}{ccc}
3154	      H^0(U, \mathcal{F}_1) & \longrightarrow & H^0(U, \mathcal{F}_2)\\
3155	      \cong\downarrow & & \downarrow\cong\\
3156	      \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_1) & \xrightarrow{\;g_U\;} & \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}_2)
3157	    \end{array}
3158	  \]
3159	  commutes, where the top arrow is \(H^0(U, g)\), the functorial action of \(g\) on
3160	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^0\) in the second variable, and the bottom arrow is the sections map
3161	  \(g_U = g(U)\).

3166	  corresponds to post-composition by \(g\) in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!
3167	  \mathcal{O}_U, -)\); under the corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr}
3168	  (which is natural in the second variable, being a composite of the sheafification adjunction and
3169	  the free–Yoneda evaluation, both natural), this in turn corresponds to applying \(g\) on sections
3170	  over \(U\), i.e. \(g_U\). Hence the square commutes. In particular, when \(g_U\) is surjective so
3171	  is \(H^0(U, g)\) — the transfer used by the surjectivity step of
3172	  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}.

3226	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on
3227	  % affines) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L145--155).
3228	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
3229	  % for quasi-coherent modules.
3230	  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
3231	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
3232	  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
3233	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
3234	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
3235	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
3236	  affines).}
3237	  Let \(U\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
3238	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Then the sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) vanishes in
3239	  all positive degrees:
3240	  \[
3241	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
3242	  \]
3243	  The formal statement carries \([\,\mathrm{EnoughInjectives}\ X.\mathrm{Modules}\,]\) as an
3244	  explicit instance hypothesis, inherited from Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.

3246	
3247	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
3248	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
3249	% As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
3250	% the affine opens of $X$.
3251	% As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
3252	% open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
3253	% Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
3254	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
3255	% hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
3256	% another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
3257	% The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
3258	% of $\mathcal{B}$, see
3259	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.
3260	% Hence (2) holds.
3261	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
3262	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
3263	\begin{proof}
3264	  \uses{def:affine_cover_system, lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh,
3265	    lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis, def:absolute_cohomology}
3266	  The proof instantiates the basis-comparison criterion
3267	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) at the affine cover system. Assemble the affine cover
3268	  system \(s\) of Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system}: its basis \(\mathcal{B}\) is the
3269	  standard (distinguished) opens of the affine \(U\) and its admissible coverings \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are
3270	  the standard open covers, the three nontrivial fields being discharged by
3271	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:affine_faces_mem}, \ref{lem:affine_surj_of_vanishing}
3272	  and~\ref{lem:affine_injective_acyclic}.
3273	
3274	  For the quasi-coherent coefficient \(\mathcal{F}\), Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh}
3275	  supplies the seed: \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(s\)
3276	  (Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}), i.e.\ condition (3) of
3277	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} holds for \(\mathcal{F}\). Applying the
3278	  basis-comparison lemma to \(s\) and \(\mathcal{F}\) yields, for every standard open
3279	  \(V \in \mathcal{B}\) and every \(p > 0\), the vanishing \(H^p(V, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Taking
3280	  \(V = U\) (the whole affine, itself a standard open) gives \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all
3281	  \(p > 0\). Throughout, the sheaf cohomology \(H^p(U, -) = \operatorname{Ext}^p(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, -)\)
3282	  is the Ext realization of Definition~\ref{def:absolute_cohomology}.
3283	\end{proof}

8141	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
8142	  If \(F, F'\) are both left adjoint to \(G\) (adjunctions \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\)), the
8143	  uniqueness isomorphism \(\operatorname{leftAdjointUniq}\mathrm{adj}_1\,\mathrm{adj}_2 : F \cong F'\)
8144	  intertwines the units: composing the unit of \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) with \(G\) applied to the
8145	  isomorphism recovers the unit of \(\mathrm{adj}_2\), i.e.\ at each object
8146	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2.\mathrm{unit} = \mathrm{adj}_1.\mathrm{unit} \circ G\bigl(
8147	  (\operatorname{leftAdjointUniq}\mathrm{adj}_1\,\mathrm{adj}_2).\mathrm{hom}\bigr)\).
8148	\end{lemma}

8150	\begin{lemma}
8151	[Per-leg coherence of the binary push--pull comparison]
8152	  \label{lem:pushPull_binary_leg_coherence}
8153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_binary_leg_coherence}
8154	  \uses{def:push_pull_map, lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
8155	    lem:unit_leftAdjointUniq_mathlib, lem:pushPullCoprodLegIso}
8156	  In the setting of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}, with \(q =
8157	  \operatorname{coprod.desc}\,Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\,Y_1.\mathrm{hom}\) and \(M = q^*\mathcal{F}\), write
8158	  \(u_0\) for the \(\operatorname{inl}\)-component of \(\operatorname{coprodDecompMap} M\) (the unit of
8159	  the restriction adjunction along \(\operatorname{inl}\) at \(M\)) and \(\mathrm{idiso}_0 :
8160	  (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{obj}\bigl(\operatorname{inl}_*(M|_{\operatorname{inl}})\bigr)
8161	  \cong \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0\) for the per-leg identification of the reference
8162	  chain. Then the \(\operatorname{inl}\)-leg of the canonical comparison factors through these:
8163	  \[
8164	    \operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,\overline{\operatorname{inl}}
8165	      \;=\; (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{map}\, u_0 \;\circ\; \mathrm{idiso}_0.\mathrm{hom},
8166	  \]
8167	  and symmetrically for the \(\operatorname{inr}\)-leg.
8168	\end{lemma}
8169	\begin{proof}
8170	  \uses{def:push_pull_map, lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
8171	    lem:unit_leftAdjointUniq_mathlib, lem:pushPullCoprodLegIso}
8172	  The two legs are symmetric; treat \(\operatorname{inl}\). Unfold the push--pull map on the
8173	  left-hand side to its raw five-step form (Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}):
8174	  \(\operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,\overline{\operatorname{inl}}\) is \(q_*\) applied to the
8175	  composite of the restriction-adjunction unit \(\eta^{\operatorname{inl}}\) at \(M\) with
8176	  \(\operatorname{inl}_*\) of the pullback-composition comparison for \(\operatorname{inl} \circ q\)
8177	  acting on \(\mathcal{F}\), followed by the over-triangle transport that \(\mathrm{idiso}_0\)
8178	  encodes. Now the open immersion \(\operatorname{inl}\) presents its restriction functor as a left
8179	  adjoint, and the comparison \(\operatorname{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\)
8180	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}) is precisely the uniqueness isomorphism
8181	  between this restriction functor and the open-immersion pullback. The unit-intertwining identity
8182	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_leftAdjointUniq_mathlib}) rewrites the adjunction unit as
8183	  \(\eta^{\operatorname{inl}} = u_0 \circ \operatorname{inl}_*\bigl(
8184	  (\operatorname{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\operatorname{inl}).\mathrm{hom}\bigr)\). Substituting and
8185	  cancelling the comparison isomorphism against the matching inverse factor inside \(\mathrm{idiso}_0\),
8186	  the residual identity is an equality of two canonical equality-transport morphisms along the single
8187	  open-set equality \(q \circ \operatorname{inl} = Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\); these agree by proof-%
8188	  irrelevance of that equality. Hence \(\operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,
8189	  \overline{\operatorname{inl}} = (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{map}\, u_0 \circ
8190	  \mathrm{idiso}_0.\mathrm{hom}\), as claimed.
8191	\end{proof}

8193	\begin{lemma}[Canonical leg identification of the binary push--pull chain]
8194	  \label{lem:pushPullCoprodLegIso}
8195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullCoprodLegIso}
8196	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
8197	  In the setting of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}, let \(c\) be one of the coproduct
8198	  inclusions \(\operatorname{inl}, \operatorname{inr} : C \to Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg Y_1.\mathrm{left}\),
8199	  with structure map \(p_C : C \to X\) and over-triangle \(w_C : c \circ q = p_C\). Writing \(M =
8200	  q^*\mathcal{F}\), there is a canonical isomorphism identifying the corresponding factor of \(q_*(P
8201	  \times Q)\) with the per-leg push--pull object:
8202	  \[
8203	    (\operatorname{pushforward} q).\mathrm{obj}\Bigl((\operatorname{pushforward} c).\mathrm{obj}
8204	      \bigl((q^*\mathcal{F}).\operatorname{restrict} c\bigr)\Bigr)
8205	      \;\cong\;
8206	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\operatorname{Over.mk} p_C).
8207	  \]
8208	\end{lemma}
8209	\begin{proof}
8210	  \uses{lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
8211	  Push forward along \(q_*\) the inner isomorphism chaining the identification of restriction with
8212	  open-immersion pullback (Lemma~\ref{lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), the
8213	  pullback-composition comparison for \(c \circ q\), and the over-triangle reindexing \(c \circ q = p_C\);
8214	  then transport the codomain along the same triangle by an equality-of-objects rewrite. This is the
8215	  per-leg identification \(\mathrm{idiso}\) of the reference chain in
8216	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}.

8219	\begin{lemma}[Splitting an $\operatorname{Option}$-indexed coproduct]
8220	  \label{lem:sigmaOptionIso}
8221	  \lean{CategoryTheory.sigmaOptionIso}
8222	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and \(Z : \operatorname{Option}\alpha \to \mathcal{C}\) a family
8223	  for which the relevant coproducts exist (the total coproduct \(\coprod Z\), the coproduct
8224	  \(\coprod_{a : \alpha} Z(\operatorname{some} a)\) over the proper part, and their binary coproduct).
8225	  Then the total coproduct splits off its \(\operatorname{none}\) summand:
8226	  \[
8227	    \coprod_{o : \operatorname{Option}\alpha} Z(o) \;\cong\; Z(\operatorname{none}) \amalg
8228	      \Bigl(\coprod_{a : \alpha} Z(\operatorname{some} a)\Bigr).
8229	  \]
8230	\end{lemma}
8231	\begin{proof}
8232	  The forward map is \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\) of the cocone sending the \(\operatorname{none}\)
8233	  component to the left binary inclusion and each \(\operatorname{some} a\) component to the composite
8234	  of the \(a\)-th inclusion into \(\coprod_{a} Z(\operatorname{some} a)\) with the right binary
8235	  inclusion. The inverse is \(\operatorname{coprod.desc}\) of the \(\operatorname{none}\) inclusion and
8236	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\) of the \(\operatorname{some}\) inclusions. The two round-trip identities
8237	  follow from the universal properties of the coproducts, checked componentwise on each
8238	  \(\operatorname{none}\) and \(\operatorname{some}\) term. (Mathlib provides the associativity
8239	  isomorphism for nested coproducts but no \(\operatorname{Option}\)-split, so this is supplied
8240	  directly.)
8241	\end{proof}

8243	\begin{lemma}[Push--pull objects transport along a slice isomorphism]
8244	  \label{lem:pushPullObjCongr}
8245	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObjCongr}
8246	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
8247	  Let \(\mathcal{F} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and let \(e : Y \cong Y'\) be an isomorphism in
8248	  \(\operatorname{Over} X\). Then the push--pull objects on \(Y\) and \(Y'\) are isomorphic,
8249	  \[
8250	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y \;\cong\; \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y'.
8251	  \]
8252	\end{lemma}
8253	\begin{proof}
8254	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
8255	  The push--pull object is a contravariant functor of its slice argument through the push--pull map
8256	  (Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}). Take the forward map to be
8257	  \(\operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,e.\mathrm{inv}\) and the backward map

8260	  push--pull map under composition and identity of over-morphisms.

8305	\begin{lemma}[Push--pull on the empty coproduct is terminal]
8306	  \label{lem:pushPull_coprod_prod_empty}
8307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_coprod_prod_empty}
8308	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf}
8309	  For the empty index type \(\iota = \operatorname{PEmpty}\) and the empty family of legs, the coproduct
8310	  \(\coprod_{i} \mathrm{legs}\,i\) is the initial scheme \(\bot\); the push--pull object on its unique
8311	  structure map is terminal in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), hence isomorphic to the empty product
8312	  \(\prod_{i \in \operatorname{PEmpty}} \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,i)\),
8313	  which is likewise terminal:
8314	  \[
8315	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,\Bigl(\operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(
8316	      \operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\Bigr)
8317	      \;\cong\;
8318	    \prod_{i \in \operatorname{PEmpty}} \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,i).
8319	  \]
8320	\end{lemma}
8321	\begin{proof}
8322	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf}
8323	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf} it suffices to check the comparison map is an
8324	  isomorphism on sections over each open \(V \subseteq X\). The source of the structure map is the
8325	  initial scheme \(\bot\), whose only open is empty; pulling \(\mathcal{F}\) back and pushing forward
8326	  along the initial map therefore gives zero sections over every \(V\) (the preimage of \(V\) in
8327	  \(\bot\) is empty). The empty product is terminal and likewise has zero sections over every \(V\).
8328	  Both sides are thus the zero presheaf of abelian groups, so the comparison is an isomorphism.
8329	\end{proof}

8331	\begin{lemma}
8332	[Push--pull on a finite coproduct of legs is the product of the leg push--pulls]
8333	  \label{lem:pushPull_coprod_prod}
8334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_coprod_prod}
8335	  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet (the finite-index induction is the
8336	  % next-session unit; the prover handed off a ~6-sub-lemma decomposition — pushPullObjCongr,
8337	  % Over-X lift of sigmaOptionIso, piOptionIso, induction_empty_option with h_empty/of_equiv/h_option,
8338	  % then the specialization). Effort-break this block before the next prover round.
8339	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod,
8340	    lem:pushPullObjCongr, lem:over_sigmaOptionIso, lem:piOptionIso, lem:pushPull_coprod_prod_empty}
8341	  Let \((\mathrm{legs} : \iota \to \operatorname{Over} X)\) be a finite family of objects of
8342	  the slice category over a scheme \(X\) (\(\iota\) finite), and form the coproduct
8343	  \(\coprod_{i \in \iota} \mathrm{legs}\,i\) with structure map
8344	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom}) : \coprod_i
8345	  \mathrm{legs}\,i \to X\). Then the push--pull object on this coproduct is the product of
8346	  the per-leg push--pull objects:
8347	  \[
8348	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,\Bigl(\operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(
8349	      \operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\Bigr)
8350	      \;\cong\;
8351	    \prod_{i \in \iota} \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,i)
8352	    \qquad \text{in } X.\mathrm{Modules}.
8353	  \]
8354	  This is the general indexed-coproduct\(\to\)product disjoint-union decomposition of a
8355	  module sheaf: the legs may have overlapping images in \(X\), but they are disjoint inside
8356	  the coproduct space \(\coprod_i \mathrm{legs}\,i\), so push--pull splits componentwise.
8357	\end{lemma}
8358	\begin{proof}
8359	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod,
8360	    lem:pushPullObjCongr, lem:over_sigmaOptionIso, lem:piOptionIso, lem:pushPull_coprod_prod_empty}
8361	  Argue by induction on the finite index type \(\iota\), using the finite-index recursion principle: a
8362	  property of finite types that is stable under index equivalence, holds for the empty type, and is
8363	  preserved by adjoining one element (passing from \(\alpha\) to \(\operatorname{Option}\alpha\)) holds
8364	  for every finite type. Throughout, the isomorphism is kept in its \emph{canonical} form as
8365	  the product of push--pull maps \(\prod_{i} \operatorname{pushPullMap}\mathcal{F}\,\overline{\iota_i}\),
8366	  where \(\overline{\iota_i}\) denotes the \(i\)-th coproduct inclusion viewed as an over-morphism
8367	  into \(\operatorname{Over.mk}(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom}))\).
8368	
8369	  \emph{Empty base.} For \(\iota = \operatorname{PEmpty}\) the claim is
8370	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_coprod_prod_empty}: both sides are terminal.
8371	
8372	  \emph{Reindexing.} For an equivalence \(\beta \simeq \alpha\), the canonical product and coproduct
8373	  framings transport along the reindexing isomorphisms of products and coproducts while preserving
8374	  the canonical form, so the property is stable under index equivalence.
8375	
8376	  \emph{Adjoining one index.} Suppose the claim holds for \(\alpha\); prove it for
8377	  \(\operatorname{Option}\alpha\). In the slice over \(X\), the coproduct over \(\operatorname{Option}
8378	  \alpha\) splits as the binary coproduct of the \(\operatorname{none}\) leg and the coproduct over
8379	  \(\alpha\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:over_sigmaOptionIso}). Transporting the push--pull object across this slice
8380	  isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPullObjCongr}) and applying the binary decomposition
8381	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}) yields the binary product of the \(\operatorname{none}\)
8382	  leg's push--pull object with the push--pull object on the \(\alpha\)-coproduct. The induction
8383	  hypothesis identifies the latter with \(\prod_{a : \alpha} \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,
8384	  (\mathrm{legs}\,(\operatorname{some} a))\), and the product splitting (Lemma~\ref{lem:piOptionIso}, in
8385	  inverse form) reassembles the binary product into the total product \(\prod_{o : \operatorname{Option}
8386	  \alpha} \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,o)\). A final check that the composite
8387	  forward map is again the canonical product of push--pull maps closes the step. (All
8388	  isomorphisms produced live in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), verified through the underlying presheaf by
8389	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf} where needed.)
8390	\end{proof}

8373	  framings transport along the reindexing isomorphisms of products and coproducts while preserving
8374	  the canonical form, so the property is stable under index equivalence.

8379	  \(\alpha\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:over_sigmaOptionIso}). Transporting the push--pull object across this slice

8382	  leg's push--pull object with the push--pull object on the \(\alpha\)-coproduct. The induction

# Blueprint-reviewer report — iter-064

## Audit scope

Whole-blueprint audit with hard-gate focus on `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` (covers both
`CechSectionIdentification.lean` and `OpenImmersionPushforward.lean`). Changes verified: Part A
(pushPull_coprod_prod decomposition) and Part B (pushforward_slice_two_adjunction decomposition +
φ'' correction).

---

## Chapter: `Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex`

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| complete | true |
| correct | true |
| must-fix | none |

Single definition `def:higher_direct_image` with `\leanok`. No open edges or broken refs.

---

## Chapter: `Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex`

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| complete | true |
| correct | true |
| must-fix | none |

All lemmas carry `\leanok` or `\mathlibok`. No structural issues.

---

## Chapter: `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| complete | **false** |
| correct | **true** |
| hard-gate verdict | **READY FOR PROVER DISPATCH** — no must-fix-this-iter findings |

### leandag diagnostics

```
unknown_uses : []       ← no broken \uses{} edges
isolated     : 2        ← pre-existing, not new
unmatched_lean: see below
```

---

## Part A — CSI route decomposition

### New Lean declarations: existence status

| Blueprint node | Lean name | In Lean? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lem:sigmaOptionIso` | `CategoryTheory.sigmaOptionIso` | **YES** | not in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:pushPullCoprodLegIso` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullCoprodLegIso` | **YES** | not in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:pushPullObjCongr` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObjCongr` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:over_sigmaOptionIso` | `AlgebraicGeometry.overSigmaOptionIso` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:piOptionIso` | `AlgebraicGeometry.piOptionIso` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:pushPull_coprod_prod_empty` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_coprod_prod_empty` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:pushPull_coprod_prod` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_coprod_prod` | YES (sorry) | has `% NOTE: build target` |

### Statement correctness — Part A

**`lem:sigmaOptionIso`**: `∐_{Option α} Z(o) ≅ Z(none) ∐ (∐_{a:α} Z(some a))`. Standard
categorical splitting. No `\uses{}` needed. Statement **CORRECT**.

**`lem:pushPullObjCongr`**: If `e : Y ≅ Y'` in `Over X` then `pushPullObj F Y ≅ pushPullObj F
Y'`. Proof: apply `pushPullMap` (contravariant). `\uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}`.
Statement **CORRECT**.

**`lem:over_sigmaOptionIso`**: The `Over X`-coproduct over `Option α` splits off the `none` leg
as a binary coproduct. `\uses{lem:sigmaOptionIso}`. Statement **CORRECT**. The proof uses
`Over.isoMk` with the underlying `sigmaOptionIso`; the structure-map compatibility is
componentwise by the coproduct universal property.

**`lem:piOptionIso`**: `∏_{Option α} W(o) ≅ W(none) × (∏_{a:α} W(some a))`. Dual of
`sigmaOptionIso`. No `\uses{}` needed. Statement **CORRECT**.

**`lem:pushPull_coprod_prod_empty`**: For `PEmpty`-indexed family, both sides are terminal.
`\uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf}`. Statement **CORRECT**. The empty
coproduct is the initial scheme; pulling back and pushing forward gives zero sections everywhere.
The empty product is terminal. Both sides are terminal in `X.Modules`.

**`lem:pushPull_coprod_prod`** (induction target): Statement `pushPullObj F (Over.mk
(Sigma.desc legs)) ≅ ∏_i pushPullObj F (legs i)` for finite `ι`. Proof by
`Fintype.induction_empty_option`: empty base (`lem:pushPull_coprod_prod_empty`), reindexing
stability, Option step (uses `lem:over_sigmaOptionIso`, `lem:pushPullObjCongr`,
`lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod`, `lem:piOptionIso`).
`\uses{...}` correctly lists all 7 dependencies. Statement **CORRECT**. Marked as build target.

**`lem:pushPullCoprodLegIso`**: Canonical per-leg identification in binary push-pull.
`\uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}`. Statement **CORRECT**.
Used by `lem:pushPull_binary_leg_coherence`. No `\leanok` visible — sync_leanok timing issue
(see §Missing `\leanok` below).

### `\uses{}` edge audit — Part A

All dependency edges for the new sub-lemmas resolve. `unknown_uses: []` confirms no broken label
references. The induction graph is:
```
pushPull_coprod_prod
├─ pushPull_coprod_prod_empty   (PEmpty base)
├─ pushPullObjCongr             (reindexing + Option transport)
├─ over_sigmaOptionIso          (Option step: slice coproduct split)
├─ piOptionIso                  (Option step: product reassembly)
├─ pushPull_binary_coprod_prod  (Option step: binary push-pull)
├─ def:push_pull_obj
└─ isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf
```
Edges correct and complete.

---

## Part B — OpenImm route decomposition and φ'' correction

### New Lean declarations: existence status

| Blueprint node | Lean name | In Lean? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lem:slice_overs_equiv_continuity` (6 decls) | see below | **YES** (all 6) | not in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:slice_reverse_ring_map` | `AlgebraicGeometry.sliceReverseRingMap` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:pushforward_slice_adjunction_h1` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardSliceAdjunctionH1` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:pushforward_slice_adjunction_h2` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardSliceAdjunctionH2` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |
| `lem:pushforward_slice_two_adjunction` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardSliceTwoAdjunction` | YES (sorry) | has `% NOTE: build target` |
| `lem:pushforward_slice_pullback_iso` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardSlicePullbackIso` | YES (sorry) | has `% NOTE: build target` |
| `lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh` | `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh` | **NO** | in unmatched_lean |

The 6 declarations covered by `lem:slice_overs_equiv_continuity`:
`opensMapHomBase_isEquivalence`, `opensEquivOfIso`, `sliceOversEquiv`,
`sliceOversEquiv_functor_isContinuous`, `overPost_slice_inverse_isContinuous`,
`sliceOversEquiv_inverse_isContinuous` — all local, consistent with iter-063 axiom-clean report.

### Statement correctness — Part B

**`lem:slice_overs_equiv_continuity`**: Opens-lattice functor is an equivalence; φ induces
`sliceOversEquiv φ Uᵢ : Over Uᵢ ≃ Over Vᵢ`; both directions are continuous.
`\uses{...}` lists 5 Mathlib anchors. Statement **CORRECT**. No `\leanok` — sync_leanok timing.

**`lem:slice_reverse_ring_map`**: The corrected φ'', defined as over-pullback of
`φ.hom.toRingCatSheafHom` along the corrected inverse `eqv.inverse =
Over.post(Opens.map φ.hom.base) ∘ Over.map(unitIso.inv)`. The statement explicitly confirms
φ'' is *object-level correction-free*: `Over.map(unitIso.inv)` leaves `.left` unchanged, so
sections over any `W : (Over Vᵢ)ᵒᵖ` are sections of `X.ringCatSheaf.over Uᵢ` over
`φ.hom⁻¹ W.left`. `\uses{lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom, lem:slice_overs_equiv_continuity}`.
Statement **CORRECT** and mathematically coherent.

**`lem:pushforward_slice_adjunction_h1`** (H₁ counit square): φ'' and ψ_r satisfy the
counit-naturality square required by `pushforwardPushforwardAdj`. Proof: both structure-sheaf
comparisons `φ.hom.toRingCatSheafHom` and `φ.inv.toRingCatSheafHom` are mutually inverse; the
two slice ring maps are their over-pullbacks; naturality collapses to an equality of
equality-transport morphisms along `φ.hom⁻¹(φ.inv⁻¹ Uᵢ) = Uᵢ`, true by proof-irrelevance.
`\uses{lem:slice_reverse_ring_map, lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}`.
Statement **CORRECT**. The proof is a proof-irrelevance argument — the right tool given the
non-definitional open identity.

**`lem:pushforward_slice_adjunction_h2`** (H₂ unit square): Same structure as H₁.
`\uses{lem:slice_reverse_ring_map, lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}`. Statement **CORRECT**.

**`lem:pushforward_slice_two_adjunction`**: `pushforward φ'' ⊣ pushforward ψ_r`. Assembled
from Mathlib's `pushforwardPushforwardAdj` applied to `(sliceOversEquiv φ Uᵢ).symm.toAdjunction`
with H₁ and H₂. The proof explicitly identifies the `Over.map(unitIso.inv)` correction as the
source of all non-definitional bookkeeping. `\uses{...}` correctly lists all 7 dependencies.
Statement **CORRECT**. Marked as build target.

**`lem:pushforward_slice_pullback_iso`**: `(pullback ψ_r).obj (H.over Uᵢ) ≅ (Φ.functor.obj
H).over Vᵢ`. Two-step proof: (1) `pullback ψ_r ≅ pushforward φ''` by left-adjoint uniqueness
(`lem:leftAdjointUniq_mathlib`), both being left adjoint to `pushforward ψ_r`; (2) sections of
`pushforward φ''` over W are definitionally `Γ(H, φ.hom⁻¹ W.left)` — the same as sections of
`(Φ.functor.obj H).over Vᵢ` over W (by `pushforward_obj_obj_mathlib`). `\uses{...}` correctly
lists 7 dependencies. Statement **CORRECT**. Marked as build target.

**`lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh`**: Pushforward of quasi-coherent H along scheme iso φ is
quasi-coherent. Proof: extract quasi-coherence datum, form image cover, use per-member
`pullback ψ_r` (left adjoint, preserves colimits, so presentations transport) and then the iso
from `lem:pushforward_slice_pullback_iso` to carry the presentation across. `\uses{...}` lists 6
dependencies. Statement **CORRECT**. Marked as build target (no `% NOTE:` annotation — see
§Soft findings below).

### φ'' correction — coherence and propagation audit

The correction is **mathematically coherent** and **consistently propagated**:

1. **Source of correction**: `Over.postEquiv F` (Mathlib) has inverse
   `Over.post F.inverse ∘ Over.map(F.unitIso.inv.app X)`. For the opens-equivalence,
   `F.inverse.obj(F.functor.obj Uᵢ) = φ.hom⁻¹(φ.inv⁻¹ Uᵢ) ≠ Uᵢ` definitionally.
   Documented in `lem:over_postEquiv_mathlib`. ✓

2. **Old φ''** (`sliceStructureSheafHom φ⁻¹ Vᵢ`): completely absent from the current blueprint.
   No stale reference found. ✓

3. **New φ''** (`sliceReverseRingMap`): consistently used in H₁, H₂, and the parent
   `pushforward_slice_two_adjunction`. ✓

4. **Propagation to `pushforward_slice_pullback_iso`**: the statement and proof reference
   `lem:slice_reverse_ring_map` and explicitly note that sections of `pushforward φ''` equal
   sections of `(Φ.functor.obj H).over Vᵢ` by preimage identity. ✓

5. **H₁/H₂ absorb the correction**: both reduce to proof-irrelevance on the open identity
   `φ.hom⁻¹(φ.inv⁻¹ Uᵢ) = Uᵢ`. This is the correct technical form for the `Over.map` factor
   being trivial at the section level. ✓

### New `\mathlibok` anchors

8 new Mathlib anchors added (directive said 4; there are 8):

| Label | Lean name | Line |
|---|---|---|
| `lem:leftAdjointUniq_mathlib` | `CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq` | 10102 |
| `lem:pushforwardPushforwardAdj_mathlib` | `SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj` | 10113 |

| `lem:opens_mapMapIso_mathlib` | `TopologicalSpace.Opens.mapMapIso` | 10146 |
| `lem:instIsContinuousOverMapOver_mathlib` | `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.instIsContinuousOverMapOver` | 10156 |
| `lem:functor_isContinuous_comp_mathlib` | `CategoryTheory.Functor.isContinuous_comp` | 10167 |
| `lem:coverPreserving_overPost_mathlib` | `CategoryTheory.CoverPreserving.overPost` | 10177 |
| `lem:pullbackPushforwardAdjunction_mathlib` | `SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction` | 10187 |

All 8 carry `\mathlibok` markers. All used correctly in `\uses{}` of the new sub-lemmas. ✓

### `\uses{}` edge audit — Part B

All edges resolve. Dependency graph for the OpenImm route:

```
pushforward_slice_pullback_iso
├─ slice_reverse_ring_map
│   ├─ slice_structureSheaf_hom
│   └─ slice_overs_equiv_continuity  (6 decls, all local)
├─ pushforward_slice_two_adjunction
